title: L1 vs. L2 in Deep Networks
concept: In deep networks, L2 usually keeps many small helpful weights alive, while L1 is more likely to zero weaker connections and create sparsity.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is L1 regularization especially good at encouraging?

options.text: Exact zero weights and sparse connections.

exercises.explain: Right. L1 stands out because it can truly switch weak connections off.

cards.title: Turn everyone down or mute a few?
cards.body: Imagine mixing a band. One option is to turn every instrument down a little so the song stays balanced. Another is to mute a few instruments entirely. L2 acts like the first option. L1 acts like the second.

cards.title: Their deepest difference
cards.body: Both L1 and L2 fight overfitting, but they shape a network differently. L2 spreads learning across many smaller weights, while L1 is more willing to zero out weaker connections and leave a leaner set of active paths.

cards.title: Budget cuts vs. across-the-board trimming
cards.body: If a team needs to save money, one strategy is that everyone takes a small cut. Another is that a few programs get canceled entirely. L2 is the small-cut strategy; L1 is the cancellation strategy.

cards.title: Many faint texture clues
cards.body: Suppose an image model uses dozens of faint texture patterns to recognize fabric. L2 often works well because it keeps many of those weak-but-real clues alive. L1 may decide some are not worth keeping and drive their connections to zero.

cards.title: Neither one is universally better
cards.body: A common myth is that one regularizer always beats the other. In practice, the better choice depends on your goal. If you want smooth shrinkage and a dense network, L2 is often the default. If you want sparsity or pruning, L1 may be better.

cards.title: Smooth shrinkage vs. sparse pruning
cards.body: Both can reduce overfitting, but they leave different weight patterns behind.

left.text:
Most weights get smaller together.
Many clues stay available.

right.text:
Some weak weights vanish completely.
The network becomes sparser.

exercises.prompt: If many tiny clues across a deep network are all somewhat useful, which regularizer is usually the safer fit?

options.text: L2, because it keeps many weights alive while shrinking them.
L1, because it is more likely to delete many of those weak clues.
Neither; regularization only works in linear regression.

exercises.explain: Exactly. L2 is often preferred when lots of weak signals matter together and you do not want aggressive pruning.

exercises.prompt: True or false: L1 is more likely than L2 to create exact zero weights.
exercises.explain: True. That tendency toward exact zeros is the main reason L1 creates sparse models.

exercises.sentence: When you want fewer active connections, ___ is usually the stronger choice.
exercises.explain: L1 is correct because it is more willing to prune connections all the way to zero.

exercises.prompt: Which description sounds more like L2 than L1?

options.text: Hundreds of connections stay active, but each becomes smaller and less extreme.
Many weak connections vanish exactly, leaving a sparse graph.
The network keeps only three non-zero weights in the whole layer.

exercises.explain: Right. That dense-but-shrunken pattern is classic L2 behavior.

exercises.prompt: A medical-image network learns from many faint texture cues that each add a little value. You want less overfitting, but you do not want to shut lots of those clues off entirely. Which choice is best?

options.text: Use L2 regularization so many small helpful connections stay alive while their sizes are controlled.
Use L1 because deleting many faint cues is exactly what you want.
Avoid regularization because any penalty would erase learning completely.
Increase a few weights dramatically so the network trusts them more.

exercises.explain: Perfect. When many small clues matter together, L2 usually matches the goal better because it shrinks without aggressively pruning.
